mappings.rationale: Directly covers the philosophy of science including scientific methodology, the status of scientific theory, empirical procedures, and the validation of theories (section B).
Section A.4.b covers the revolution in natural philosophy by Bacon, Descartes, and Galileo, which is the core of the Scientific Revolution.
Section B.6 addresses the relevance of scientific knowledge to other spheres of human experience, including the long historical tension between science and religion.
Astronomy's development is a central thread in the history of science from ancient Greek through modern times, covered throughout section A.
Galileo is specifically named in section A.4.b.i as a key figure in the radical reformulation of natural knowledge during the scientific revolution.
Newton's work is foundational to sections A.4 and B.2.c (the Kantian interpretation of Newtonian physics), representing a pivotal moment in the history of science.
Darwin's theory of evolution profoundly transformed 19th-century science (section A.6) and raised enduring questions in the philosophy of science.
The Copernican revolution is part of the rebirth of science in the Renaissance (section A.4.a) and the revolution in natural philosophy.
Faraday's contributions to electromagnetism and experimental science exemplify the 19th-century progress in physics discussed in section A.6.
Bohr's work on quantum mechanics is central to the profound changes in natural sciences that drove 20th-century philosophy of science debates (section B.2.e).
The history of physics is a major component of the history of science covered in section A, from Greek science through modern developments.
Chemistry's development is part of the broader history of science in section A, including the institutional organization of science.
Medicine's history is part of the broader development of science discussed in sections A.5 through A.7, including institutional and social aspects.
Section A.2.a covers science in Greek civilization including protoscience, disciplined observation, and the Platonic vs. Aristotelian view of nature.
Descartes is named in sections A.4.b.i and B.2.b as central to the revolution in natural philosophy and the debate about scientific methodology.
Aristotle's empirical approach to natural science is discussed in section A.2.a.ii and his influence on the Platonic-Aristotelian debate in the philosophy of science.
Section A.2.a.ii covers the Platonic view of nature and its contrast with the Aristotelian approach, foundational to the philosophy of science.
Pavlov exemplifies the social organization and common pattern of advance in early 20th-century science discussed in section A.7.
Eugenics illustrates the moral and political difficulties facing science discussed in section A.8 on contemporary problems and prospects.
Section B.5.b discusses the application of different philosophical doctrines to different sciences, and biology raises unique philosophical questions.
Section B.2.e covers 20th-century responses to relativity, quantum mechanics, and other changes in natural sciences, which are the core of philosophy of physics.
The demarcation between science and pseudoscience is a central problem in the philosophy of science, relating to sections B.3 and B.4 on scientific validation.
Section A.3 covers science in medieval Western civilization, where Aquinas's synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy with theology shaped the intellectual context.
Hume's empiricism is central to section B.2.c on 18th-century philosophy of science, particularly the problem of induction and scientific methodology.
Section B.2.c-d covers Kantian interpretation of Newtonian physics and the belief in the unique rationality of the classical synthesis, central to philosophy of science.
Section A.5.b covers the intellectual origins of revolution in the spirit of the Enlightenment, which shaped the relationship between science and society.
Objectivity is a central concern of the philosophy of science, relating to sections B.3-4 on empirical procedures, validation, and the status of scientific theory.
Causation is a fundamental concept in scientific explanation, relevant to sections B.3 on empirical procedures and B.4 on scientific discovery and validation.
The measurement and conceptualization of time is part of the history of science and its empirical methods discussed throughout section A.
Section B.3.b specifically lists measurement as a key empirical procedure of science.
The relationship between scientific knowledge and religious belief is addressed in section B.6 on the relevance of science to other spheres of human experience.
The philosophical implications of scientific knowledge for religious belief relate to section B.6 on science's relevance to other spheres of experience.
Skepticism as a philosophical tradition informs the philosophy of science, particularly regarding the validation and justification of theories in section B.4.
Section B.2.e discusses relativity as one of the profound changes driving 20th-century debate in the philosophy of science.
Section B.2.e discusses quantum mechanics as a profound change driving 20th-century philosophical debates, including Logical Positivism vs. Neo-Kantianism.
